1: The priestly divisions (maḥlĕqôt) of Aaron’s sons. The chapter opens by noting that Aaron had four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. The four names are standard (Exod 6:23; Lev 10:1; Num 3:2). The mention of all four before the narrative of Nadab and Abihu’s death prepares the reader to understand why only 24 courses (not more) emerge from the Aaronic line.
2: wayyāmutû nadāḇ wăʾăḇîhû lipnê ʾăḇîhem — ‘Nadab and Abihu died before their father.’ The reference is to Lev 10:1-3 (they offered unauthorized fire, ʾêš zārāh) and Num 3:4. lipnê ʾăḇîhem (before their father, i.e., while Aaron still lived) is both chronological (they pre-deceased Aaron) and possibly theological (their death ‘before the face of their father’ is a judgment that strips the priestly succession from their line). They ‘left no sons’ (ûḇānîm lōʾ-hāyû lāhem), so the entire Aaronic succession devolves to Eleazar and Ithamar.
3: David with Zadok (ṣāḏôq) from Eleazar’s line and Ahimelech (ʾăḥîmelek) from Ithamar’s line assigned them (wayyeḥallêqêm) according to their appointed duties (kĕpêquddātām). The Hiphil wayyeḥallêqêm (he distributed them) echoes the Levitical distribution of ch. 23. pêquddāh (appointed duty, office) is from pāqaḏ (to appoint, number, visit). The two high-priestly families (Zadokites and Ithamarites) share the organizational work under David’s authority.
4: More family heads were found among Eleazar’s descendants than among Ithamar’s, so sixteen heads went to Eleazar, eight to Ithamar. The numerical asymmetry (16:8 = 2:1 ratio) reflects actual demographic reality within the Aaronic line — Eleazar’s descendants were more numerous. The proportion is observed and honored in the organizational structure; the Chronicler does not artificially equalize the assignments.
5: wayyĕḥallêqûm bêgôrālôt — ‘they were assigned by lots.’ gôrāl (lot) is the instrument of divine determination throughout the OT (Lev 16:8-10; Josh 14-21 for land distribution; Prov 16:33 ‘the lot is cast into the lap, but every decision is from YHWH’). The lots ensure that the assignment of priestly courses is YHWH’s choice, not a product of David’s favoritism or political calculation. Both Eleazar’s and Ithamar’s descendants are leaders (śārê qōḏeš wĕśārê hāʾĕlōhîm, ‘leaders of the sanctuary and leaders of God’) regardless of numerical proportion.
6: Shemaiah son of Nethaneel, a Levitical scribe (hassōpêr hallêwî), records the assignments before the king, the princes, Zadok, Ahimelech, and the heads of the priestly and Levitical families. The presence of a scribe (sōpêr) ensures the written, legal permanence of the lot assignments. The audience of witnesses (king, princes, priests, Levites) gives the assignments binding authority across all of Israel’s institutional sectors. This bureaucratic precision is characteristic of the Chronicler’s administrative concern.
7: The first lot (haggôrāl hārîšôn) falls to Jehoiarib (yĕhôyārîḇ); the second to Jedaiah (yĕḏaʿyāh). Jehoiarib means ‘YHWH pleads/contends’; the priestly name encodes the theology of YHWH as Israel’s advocate. In the post-exilic period, Jehoiarib became one of the most prominent priestly families (Neh 11:10; 1 Macc 2:1 — Mattathias the Maccabee is ‘from the sons of Joarib,’ making the Hasmonean dynasty descendants of the first priestly course).
8: Third: Harim; fourth: Seorim. The courses continue. Harim means ‘snub-nosed’ or ‘flat-nosed’ — a descriptive physical name (cf. Lev 21:18 where the same term excludes from priestly ministry those with physical defects). The priestly course bearing this name presumably did not associate the name with the disability; it had become a clan designation. Ezra 2:39 and Neh 7:42 list the sons of Harim among the priestly returnees from Babylon.
9: Fifth: Malchijah; sixth: Mijamin. Malchijah means ‘my king is YHWH’ — a theophoric name asserting YHWH’s royal sovereignty. Mijamin means ‘from the right hand’ — the position of honor and power (Ps 110:1 ‘sit at my right hand’). The theophoric names woven into the priestly courses reflect the covenantal theology of their bearers’ families.
10: Seventh: Hakkoz (haqqôṣ); eighth: Abijah (ʾăḇîyāh). Abijah is the eighth priestly course. This is the direct background of Luke 1:5: ‘In the time of Herod king of Judea there was a priest named Zechariah, who belonged to the priestly division of Abijah.’ The 24 priestly courses established here were still operative in the Second Temple period. Luke 1:8-9 specifies that Zechariah was ministering ‘when his division was on duty.’ The lot that fell to Abijah in David’s organization (v10) is the specific providential structure that placed Zechariah in the temple at the moment of Gabriel’s appearance. The eighth course = the eighth two-week rotation of the year.
11: Ninth: Jeshua (yêšûaʿ); tenth: Shecaniah (šĕḵanyāhû). yêšûaʿ is the same name as Joshua and as Jesus (Gk. Iēsous) — ‘YHWH saves.’ The ninth priestly course bears the name that will become the personal name of the great High Priest who supersedes all earthly priestly courses (Heb 7:26-27).
12: Eleventh: Eliashib (ʾĕlyāšîḇ); twelfth: Jakim (yāqîm). Eliashib means ‘God restores’ and became the name of a prominent high priest in Nehemiah’s time (Neh 3:1; 12:10; 13:4,7) who unfortunately compromised the temple precincts by allowing Tobiah an enemy of Israel to occupy rooms there. The name that means ‘God restores’ belongs to a high priest who facilitated desecration.
13: Thirteenth: Huppah (ḥuppāh); fourteenth: Jeshebeab (yĕšĕḇʾāḇ). ḥuppāh may relate to ḥuppāh (canopy, wedding chamber — Joel 2:16; Ps 19:5), giving the priestly course a name associated with sacred festivity and marriage. The names of the later courses are less theologically freighted and more likely functional or geographic clan identifiers.
14: Fifteenth: Bilgah (bilgāh); sixteenth: Immer (ʾimmêr). Immer is a prominent priestly family: Jer 20:1 (Pashhur son of Immer, who struck Jeremiah); Ezra 2:37 (1,052 sons of Immer return from exile); Neh 11:13. The Immer priestly family had significant presence in the exile period and afterward.
15: Seventeenth: Hezir (ḥêzîr); eighteenth: Aphses (happiṣṣêṣ). The name Hezir appears in Neh 10:20 among those who sealed the covenant under Ezra/Nehemiah — confirming the continuity of these priestly courses through the exile and return.
16: Nineteenth: Pethahiah (pêtaḥyāhû); twentieth: Jehezekel (yeḥezqêʾl). Jehezekel (= Ezekiel) means ‘God strengthens.’ The priestly course name and the great exilic prophet share the same name, though the course was established long before Ezekiel the prophet.
17: Twenty-first: Jachin (yāḵîn); twenty-second: Gamul (gāmûl). yāḵîn means ‘he will establish/make firm’ — the same name as the right pillar of Solomon’s temple (1 Kgs 7:21; 2 Chr 3:17). The priestly course shares its name with the temple’s founding architectural element, a conjunction of nomenclature that was presumably intentional or at least noticed by those who served in the course.
18: Twenty-third: Delaiah (dêlayāhû); twenty-fourth: Maaziah (maʿazyāhû). Maaziah means ‘YHWH is refuge/strength.’ The twenty-fourth course closes the cycle — the system then rotates back to Jehoiarib. Twenty-four is the number that will structure the priestly courses, the Levitical musical courses (ch. 25), the Levitical gatekeepers, and the elders around the throne in Rev 4:4 (twenty-four elders). The number encodes the complete institutional structure of Israel’s covenant service.
19: ʾêlleh pêquddātām lăḇôʾām lĕḇêt YHWH kĕmišpāṭām — ‘These were their appointed duties for entering the LORD’s house according to their prescribed procedure.’ pêquddāh (appointed duty) and mišpāṭ (procedure, right, judgment) together frame the system as both divinely appointed (pāqaḏ) and legally ordered (mišpāṭ). The procedure traces back to ‘Aaron their father, as YHWH the God of Israel had commanded him’ (kaʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾêl) — connecting the Davidic organization directly to the Mosaic command.
20: The remaining Levites not in the 24 priestly courses: from Amram’s line, Shubael; from Shubael’s line, Jehdeiah. This second list (vv20-31) enumerates the Levites organized alongside the priestly courses by the same lot process (v31). The structural symmetry — priests organized by lot, Levites organized by lot — ensures that the entire institutional leadership is appointed by YHWH’s determination.
21: From Rehabiah’s line: Isshiah the head (rôš). Rehabiah was the son of Eliezer the son of Moses (23:17) — the line of Moses continues in temple administration. The Mosaic descendants do not become priests (only Aaronic sons serve at the altar) but they hold prominent Levitical leadership positions, maintaining the honor of Moses’s lineage within the temple system.
22: From the Izharite line: Shelomoth; from Shelomoth’s line, Jahath (yaḥat). The Izharites are Kohathites descended from Izhar, brother of Amram. Their temple role focuses on treasury duties (1 Chr 26:25-28).
23: Hebron’s sons listed in order: Jeriah the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, Jekameam the fourth. This corresponds to 23:19 — the list is reproduced to assign the Hebronites their specific lot position within the broader Levitical rotation. The parallel structure between ch. 23 (clan list) and ch. 24 (lot assignments) is the Chronicler’s characteristic method of cross-referencing within his institutional history.
24: From Uzziel’s line: Michah; from Michah’s line, Shamir (šāmîr). šāmir means ‘thorn, flint, emery stone’ — a hard-stone name suggesting endurance or sharpness. The Uzzielites continue through the post-exilic period as an identifiable Levitical family.
25: Michah’s brother was Isshiah; Isshiah’s son was Zechariah (zĕḵaryāhû). Zechariah means ‘YHWH remembers’ — one of the most common theophoric names in the OT, appearing 30+ times for different individuals including the post-exilic prophet Zechariah whose book closes with the vision of YHWH as king over all the earth (Zech 14:9).
26: Sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. And from Jaaziah (yaʿazîyāhû) — another of Merari’s descendants — his son Beno (bênô, ‘his son’). Beno may be a personal name that happens to look like the common noun bên (son), or the text may be using ‘his son’ as a placeholder for a name not preserved. The LXX and some commentators read Beno as a proper name; the MT is ambiguous.
27: Jaaziah’s descendants: Beno, Shoham (šōham), Zaccur (zakkûr), and Ibri (ʿiḇrî). ʿiḇrî means ‘Hebrew’ or ‘one who crosses over’ — a Levite whose name is the adjective-form of Israel’s national identity. The name may preserve a tradition of this branch’s particular identity or role.
28: Mahli’s son Eleazar had no sons (lōʾ-hāyû lô bānîm) — a second instance of a priestly/Levitical line ending without male heirs (cf. 23:22). The repeated notation of heirless deaths without resolution (in 23:22 the daughters marry cousins; here no solution is mentioned) reflects honest genealogical record-keeping rather than theological idealization.
29: Kish’s line: his son Jerahmeel (yĕraḥmĕʾêl). Jerahmeel means ‘God will have compassion/mercy’ (rāḥam + ʾêl). The Jerahmeelites appear in 1 Sam 27:10 as a people in the southern Negev, possibly a non-Israelite group absorbed into Judah. This Merarite Jerahmeel is a Levitical official, not connected to the Negev Jerahmeelites.
30: Mushi’s sons: Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth (yĕrîmôt). ‘These were the Levites organized by their ancestral families’ (ʾēlleh bênê hallêwî lĕḇêt ʾăḇōtām) — the closing formula for the Levitical section before the lot-casting of v31.
31: ‘These also cast lots (wayyapîlû gam-hêmmāh gôrālôt) alongside their priestly brothers the sons of Aaron, before King David, Zadok, Ahimelech, and the heads of the priestly and Levitical families.’ The symmetry is deliberate: just as the priestly courses are determined by lot, so are the Levitical assignments. The lot places both priests and Levites under divine rather than human appointment. gam-hêmmāh (they also) signals the inclusive equity: the same divine process governs all Israel’s institutional leadership, ‘the head of the family as well as his younger brother alike’ (kāḵ lĕqāṭōn kĕgāḏôl rāš-ʾāḇ kêʾāḥîw).
